title: Saptapadi
subtitle: Seven Steps Around the Fire
synopsis: A powerful exploration of love, identity, and societal norms through the metaphor of the seven sacred vows of marriage. When a hijra is found murdered in Bangalore, a university professor is drawn into a web of secrets that challenges everything she believed about justice.

title: Adbhut
subtitle: The Wonder Within
synopsis:
A solo Gujarati adaptation of Every Brilliant Thing by Duncan Macmillan and Jonny Donahoe — one of the most performed plays in the world — reimagined for Indian audiences by Satchit Puranik and directed by Manoj Shah.

The original play premiered at the Ludlow Fringe Festival in 2013 with Donahoe performing, before transferring to London and an HBO special. It has since been staged thousands of times worldwide. Puranik's Gujarati adaptation transforms the play's cultural texture — Doordarshan evenings replace Saturday morning cartoons, garba nights replace school dances — while preserving its radical theatrical form: the audience is the cast.

From the moment the play begins, the boundary between performer and audience dissolves. RJ Devaki guides the room through the story of a girl who, at age seven, begins a list of every brilliant thing worth living for as a way to pull her mother back from the edge of depression. The list grows as the girl grows, through adolescence, heartbreak, marriage, and the quiet weight of adulthood.

With nothing but a chair, a Daffy Duck sweatshirt, and extraordinary presence, Devaki turns strangers in the audience into co-performers, drawing out laughter, silence, and tears in equal measure. Adbhut is not a lecture on mental health — it is a lived experience of it, told with the warmth of someone who understands that sadness and wonder can occupy the same breath.

title: Adhe Adhure
subtitle: Halfway House
synopsis: Mohan Rakesh's masterpiece about a middle-class family disintegrating under the weight of unfulfilled desires and unspoken truths. A wife and mother searches for completeness in the men around her, only to find that wholeness must come from within.

title: Kaumudi
subtitle: Moonlight on the Ganges
synopsis: Set against the backdrop of the Indian independence movement, Kaumudi follows a young woman's journey from a traditional Gujarati household to the frontlines of the freedom struggle. A sweeping story of courage, sacrifice, and the price of liberty.

title: Chanakya
subtitle: The Kingmaker
synopsis: The story of ancient India's greatest political strategist, told through the lens of modern power dynamics. Chanakya's ruthless brilliance shaped an empire — but at what personal cost?

title: Mughal-e-Azam
subtitle: The Musical
synopsis: A grand musical adaptation of the iconic love story between Prince Salim and Anarkali, featuring lavish sets, live orchestral music, and choreography that brings the Mughal court to breathtaking life.

title: Broken Images
synopsis: A solo performance where an acclaimed writer confronts her own video image, which begins to challenge and interrogate her. A riveting exploration of identity, authenticity, and the masks we wear.

title: Toba Tek Singh
synopsis: Based on Saadat Hasan Manto's legendary short story about the exchange of lunatics between India and Pakistan during Partition. A devastating commentary on borders, belonging, and the madness of nations.

title: Jasma Odan
subtitle: The Fragrance of Jasmine
synopsis: A reimagining of the beloved Gujarati folk tale of Jasma, a woman of extraordinary beauty and fierce independence. Set in rural Kutch, the production weaves traditional music and dance into a story of resistance and dignity.
commissionContext: Commissioned for the centenary celebration of Gujarati folk theatre.

title: Bhamasha
subtitle: The Warrior Who Funded Freedom
synopsis:
The story of Bhamashah — born 1547, Jain Shravak of the Oswal community, warrior, strategist, and the man who bankrolled Maharana Pratap's war against the Mughal empire. Little is known about him beyond the legend of his donation, but this play recovers the full arc of his life: his father Bharmal's service as fort commander of Ranthambore, his own rise as Diwan of Mewar, his raids on Mughal camps, and the moment at the village of Chulia when he presented twenty lakh gold coins and two-and-a-half crore silver rupees to a king with no kingdom left.

Ideas Unlimited — known for dramatising biographies in Gujarati — chose to tell this story in Hindi, bringing it to the Tata Theatre at NCPA on Republic Day 2013. The story was conceived by Dr. Bipin Doshi and dramatised by Mihir Bhuta. Dayashankar Pandey, who had appeared in Lagaan and Swades, took on the title role, working on the Rajasthani dialect to inhabit a man who proved that ahimsa does not mean cowardice. Om Puri lent his voice as narrator. Chugge Khan and his ensemble of Manganiar folk musicians from Rajasthan performed the Marwari folk score live, transporting the audience four centuries into the past.

Bhamasha is a play about a question most histories ignore: who pays for freedom? Not the king on the battlefield, but the man behind him — the merchant, the strategist, the Jain who believed that protecting his land was as sacred as any vow of non-violence.

title: Bhav Prapanch
subtitle: The Soul's Journey Through Worldly Illusion
synopsis:
Based on the Upamitibhavaprapancha Katha by Siddharshi Gani — a 10th-century Sanskrit text that Hermann Jacobi called the first allegorical novel in Indian literature, predating Bunyan's Pilgrim's Progress by seven centuries.

The play follows Dramak, an ordinary soul trapped in the city of Adrishyamool Paryant — a place that stands for the world as we know it, governed by greed, attachment, illusion, and desire. Dramak lives a bestial existence, wasting his time in futile efforts to preserve his meagre possessions. Caught in the bonds of attachment and illusion, believing the worldly illusion around him to be truth, he accumulates infinite karma and moves further from liberation with each birth and death.

But there exists a Maharaja's royal temple — maintained with skill and devotion by servants who guide souls toward spiritual liberation through dharma, renunciation, compassion, and mercy. Their hands are always extended. The question is whether Dramak will take hold.

The concept was distilled from Siddharshi's 16,000-verse original by Dr. Jitendra B. Shah, Director of the L.D. Institute of Indology, and dramatised by Prathang Dave and Raju Dave. Kabir Thakore designed the sets and Kanhaiya composed the music. The tagline reads: the end of sorrow is the beginning of peaceful happiness.

title: Court Martial
synopsis: Based on Swadesh Deepak's searing play about a Dalit soldier court-martialed for killing his superior officers. A tense courtroom drama that peels back layers of caste discrimination within the Indian Army.

title: Achalayatan
subtitle: Rabindranath Tagore's Allegory of Freedom
synopsis:
Tagore's searing allegory of an ashram so rigid that even light is forbidden entry. A young student named Panchak, unable to cope with the dead weight of rules, peeks through a forbidden window and discovers a world outside — free, simple, alive. His encounter with the untouchables beyond the walls sets him on a path of self-discovery that challenges the very foundations of the institution. When the Guruji finally arrives, he does not repair the system — he breaks it open.

Adapted into Gujarati by Giridhar Kripalani under the guidance of Kaka Kalelkar, this production was Ideas Unlimited's tribute to India's first Nobel laureate. Manoj Shah spent eight months — six in study, two in rehearsal — preparing the play, calling it one of Tagore's best crafted works. The production premiered at the five-day Tagore Festival in Kolkata at G. D. Birla Sabhagar, where it was the only Gujarati-language play presented — a bold choice that brought Tagore's most scathing critique of institutional orthodoxy back to his own city, in a language he never wrote in.

title: Sangeet Baari
subtitle: The Music Room
synopsis: Set in the fading grandeur of a Maharashtrian landlord's music room, this production blends classical Indian music with contemporary theatre to tell a story of art, obsession, and decline.
